chunks.title: Vibe Pillars
chunks.text: Vibe Pillars Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/00 abstract Source Draft Vibe Pillars Abstract This paper argues that permissionless perpetuals for low cap and newly launched tokens are constrained by three coupled design problems. The first is bootstrap and counterparty formation : thin markets cannot rely on continuous synchronous matching, so the system must use asynchronous matching and still provide a reliable counterparty when natural long and short flow does not exist at the same time. The second is exploit resistance : once a market uses protocol backed credit through margin to support that asynchronous structure, it must ensure the system cannot be abused through manipulation, insolvency, or uncontained tail risk. The third is LP yield and capital efficiency : once a solver or designated risk taker stands in as the counterparty, that capital must earn enough return to remain in the system. These three pillars are often treated separately, but in practice they define a single architectural problem. Order books solve parts of it for deep, mature assets because every trade is synchronously matched against a natural counterparty. That model does not generalize to the long tail. Low cap markets are intermittent, path dependent, and too thin to assume

chunks.title: Vibe Pillars
chunks.text: for deep, mature assets because every trade is synchronously matched against a natural counterparty. That model does not generalize to the long tail. Low cap markets are intermittent, path dependent, and too thin to assume continuous two sided flow. Vibe's architecture should therefore be understood as a response to this exact constraint set. It aims to build a perp venue that can bootstrap markets without waiting for perfect synchronous demand, defend the protocol once asynchronous matching requires protocol backed credit, and remain sufficiently capital efficient that liquidity providers and risk bearers can earn durable yield. The protocol's design is not arbitrary. It is the consequence of solving these three pillars at once. Version 1.0 — March 2026

chunks.title: Introduction
chunks.text: Introduction Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/01 introduction Source Draft Introduction The dream of permissionless perpetuals sounds simple: let any token have a derivative market. In practice, this is one of the hardest products in crypto to build well, especially for low cap assets. The reason is structural. A perpetual market is not just a price chart with leverage attached. It is a credit system. Traders receive exposure through margin. Counterparties absorb risk. Liquidity providers or market makers stand behind the market during periods of stress. If any part of this chain is weak, the venue either becomes exploitable, unbootstrappable, or uneconomic. For mature assets with deep order flow, the problem is partly hidden. Order books benefit from synchronous matching: a long is matched with a short, and the market can rely on dense, continuous activity. This naturally reduces some bootstrap problems because the counterparty is often another trader rather than the system itself. Low cap markets do not have that luxury. Demand is fragmented across time. Longs and shorts do not always arrive together. Volume may be episodic rather than continuous. In those conditions, a protocol cannot simply copy the architecture used by mature markets and expect it to

chunks.title: Introduction
chunks.text: across time. Longs and shorts do not always arrive together. Volume may be episodic rather than continuous. In those conditions, a protocol cannot simply copy the architecture used by mature markets and expect it to work. This paper frames Vibe through three core pillars: 1. Exploit resistance : how to keep a leveraged system from being abused. 2. Bootstrap and counterparty formation : how to keep a market tradable when natural matching is weak. 3. LP yield and capital efficiency : how to ensure the parties taking residual risk are paid enough to remain. The key insight is that these pillars are not modular choices. They constrain one another. Solving one pillar poorly tends to make another impossible. Vibe is best understood as an architecture designed around this coupled problem rather than around a generic desire to launch more perp markets. Back to Overview Next: Pillar One →

chunks.title: Pillar One: Exploit Resistance
chunks.text: Pillar One: Exploit Resistance Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/02 pillar one exploit resistance Source Draft Pillar One: Exploit Resistance Any margin system creates a basic question: how do you stop users from extracting value from the protocol faster than the system can defend itself? This is the first pillar because perpetuals necessarily extend credit. Traders post collateral and receive leveraged exposure. That means the protocol is always vulnerable to bad debt, manipulation, delayed liquidation, and edge cases where price or liquidity moves faster than the defense system. For Vibe, exploit resistance is not just about preventing hacks. It is about making sure the market structure itself cannot be gamed: leverage must remain bounded by credible collateral liquidations must happen before losses become socialized oracle and execution design must minimize manipulation surfaces the protocol must survive tail events without becoming insolvent Traditional order books solve part of this by requiring natural counterparties at the moment of execution. Because the system does not need to warehouse much residual exposure itself, some risks are reduced. But that solution depends on continuous synchronous liquidity, which low cap assets rarely have. So the real question is not whether exploit resistance matters. It is how

chunks.title: Pillar One: Exploit Resistance
chunks.text: much residual exposure itself, some risks are reduced. But that solution depends on continuous synchronous liquidity, which low cap assets rarely have. So the real question is not whether exploit resistance matters. It is how to preserve exploit resistance in a market design that also wants to serve thin and asynchronous assets. That is why margin architecture, liquidation logic, and risk containment sit underneath everything else. If the venue can be abused, there is no second pillar and no third pillar. The market dies before bootstrap or yield even matter. ← Introduction Next: Pillar Two →

chunks.title: Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty
chunks.text: Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/03 pillar two bootstrap and counterparty Source Draft Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty The second pillar is the hardest practical problem: how do you make a market exist before it is already liquid? Every perpetual trade needs a counterparty. In a deep order book, that counterparty can usually be found through synchronous matching. A long meets a short at the same time, the trade clears, and the market functions without the protocol needing to invent liquidity on its own. But low cap markets are different. Flow is sparse, volumes are low, and interest is highly path dependent. There may be strong demand for a market in aggregate, but not enough continuous two sided flow to sustain an order book at every moment. That makes synchronous matching a poor default design for the long tail. This is where Vibe's bootstrap logic matters. If natural counterparties do not arrive together, a solver or designated risk taker must often intermediate the market. That creates continuity where an order book would stall. The payoff is obvious: markets can launch earlier assets do not need to wait for deep organic liquidity users can access exposure

chunks.title: Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty
chunks.text: often intermediate the market. That creates continuity where an order book would stall. The payoff is obvious: markets can launch earlier assets do not need to wait for deep organic liquidity users can access exposure without requiring perfect timing from the opposite side But this also changes the system's economics. The moment a solver steps in as the effective counterparty, the protocol no longer has a purely matching problem. It now has a balance sheet problem. That is why bootstrap and exploit resistance are linked. You cannot just add a counterparty of convenience. You need a counterparty design that remains robust under leverage, stress, and thin conditions. The long tail thesis therefore depends on accepting an uncomfortable truth: if you want to list markets before they are mature, you must solve asynchronous counterparty formation directly. ← Pillar One Next: Pillar Three →

chunks.title: Pillar Three: LP Yield and Capital Efficiency
chunks.text: Pillar Three: LP Yield and Capital Efficiency Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/04 pillar three lp yield and capital efficiency Source Draft Pillar Three: LP Yield and Capital Efficiency Once a system solves bootstrap by introducing a solver, maker, or designated residual counterparty, a third problem appears immediately: why should that capital stay? This is the capital efficiency pillar. Someone in the system is taking risk, warehousing inventory, or funding the stable collateral side of the market. If the return on that capital is weak, the market may technically function, but it will not scale or persist. This is especially important in low cap perpetuals. The long tail cannot support architectures that require large amounts of idle capital earning weak or inconsistent yield. If the protocol needs too much balance sheet to bootstrap each market, the model breaks under its own cost. So the relevant question is not simply whether LPs can earn yield. It is whether they can earn enough yield relative to the risk they take: enough to justify residual counterparty exposure enough to compensate for thin and volatile markets enough to keep capital from fleeing to simpler opportunities This is why capital efficiency is inseparable from the

chunks.title: Pillar Three: LP Yield and Capital Efficiency
chunks.text: risk they take: enough to justify residual counterparty exposure enough to compensate for thin and volatile markets enough to keep capital from fleeing to simpler opportunities This is why capital efficiency is inseparable from the first two pillars. A highly safe system that cannot pay its makers is not viable. A highly bootstrappable system that burns too much capital per market is not viable either. Vibe's challenge is therefore to make the bootstrap layer economically attractive without recreating the inefficiencies of designs that rely on massive passive stablecoin liquidity. The protocol must concentrate risk where it can be managed well, rather than spreading capital thinly across every listed market. In short, yield is not a marketing feature. It is the economic proof that the architecture can survive. ← Pillar Two Next: The Coupled Problem →

chunks.title: The Coupled Design Problem
chunks.text: The Coupled Design Problem Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/05 the coupled design problem Source Draft The Coupled Design Problem The three pillars are easiest to misunderstand when treated as independent checkboxes. They are not. They form a coupled design problem: If you optimize only for exploit resistance, you may end up with a market so restrictive that low cap assets cannot bootstrap. If you optimize only for bootstrap, you may introduce residual counterparties that are easy to exploit or too expensive to sustain. If you optimize only for LP yield, you may over financialize the system and expose makers to unacceptable tail risk. This is why the transcript's underlying intuition is so important: most existing systems solve these issues by collapsing them into synchronous matching. A long and a short arrive together, so the market gets a counterparty and the protocol avoids warehousing much exposure itself. That works well for mature assets. It does not solve the long tail. Low cap markets create a different requirement set: 1. The protocol must defend against abuse in a leveraged environment. 2. The protocol must remain tradable even when buyers and sellers do not appear at the same moment. 3. The residual

chunks.title: The Coupled Design Problem
chunks.text: different requirement set: 1. The protocol must defend against abuse in a leveraged environment. 2. The protocol must remain tradable even when buyers and sellers do not appear at the same moment. 3. The residual risk takers must earn enough on capital to justify participating. Seen through that lens, Vibe is not just another perp venue. It is an attempt to build a market structure that can operate where order books become structurally weak: the early, thin, and path dependent phase of asset life. That is also why comparisons to traditional order books can be misleading. Order books are not wrong. They are simply optimized for a different point on the market maturity curve. Vibe's role is to solve the earlier stage where the three pillars are hardest to satisfy simultaneously. ← Pillar Three Next: Conclusion →

chunks.title: Conclusion
chunks.text: Conclusion Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/06 conclusion Source Draft Conclusion Vibe's architecture can be summarized through three pillars: 1. It must be hard to exploit. 2. It must make markets tradable before continuous two sided flow exists. 3. It must generate enough yield for the parties supplying risk capital. Each pillar is necessary. None is sufficient on its own. This is what makes permissionless perpetuals for low cap assets a distinct market design problem rather than a simple extension of existing perp venues. Mature markets can lean on synchronous matching and deep liquidity. Early markets cannot. They need a structure that can safely intermediate risk, bootstrap counterparties, and do so with enough capital efficiency to remain attractive. That is the real meaning of the Vibe thesis. The protocol is not defined only by what it lists. It is defined by the architectural constraints it solves. If those three pillars hold, the long tail becomes tradable. If they fail, permissionless perpetuals remain stuck serving only the assets that were already liquid enough to succeed without them. ← The Coupled Design Problem Back to Overview

chunks.title: Vibe Pillars
chunks.text: Vibe Pillars Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/10 vibe pillars/10 docs/overview Source Draft Vibe Pillars Paper Overview This paper explains the three core pillars Vibe must solve to make permissionless perpetual markets viable for low cap and newly launched tokens. The central argument is that these are not separate design questions but a coupled system: 1. The protocol must resist exploitation in a leveraged environment. 2. The protocol must provide a reliable counterparty even when natural two sided flow is weak. 3. The protocol must offer enough yield and capital efficiency for liquidity providers and risk bearers to stay in the system. Traditional order books partially solve these issues for mature assets through synchronous matching, but they break down at the long tail edge where markets are thin, fragmented, and not continuously active. Vibe's hybrid solver architecture exists to solve exactly that gap. Table of Contents Section File Description Abstract 00 Abstract.md Executive summary of the three pillar thesis 1. Introduction 01 Introduction.md Why low cap perpetuals are structurally hard 2. Pillar One: Exploit Resistance 02 Pillar One Exploit Resistance.md Margin, leverage, and protocol safety 3. Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty 03 Pillar Two Bootstrap and Counterparty.md Why low cap markets cannot rely

chunks.title: Vibe Pillars
chunks.text: structurally hard 2. Pillar One: Exploit Resistance 02 Pillar One Exploit Resistance.md Margin, leverage, and protocol safety 3. Pillar Two: Bootstrap and Counterparty 03 Pillar Two Bootstrap and Counterparty.md Why low cap markets cannot rely on synchronous matching 4. Pillar Three: LP Yield and Capital Efficiency 04 Pillar Three LP Yield and Capital Efficiency.md How makers must be paid without breaking the system 5. The Coupled Design Problem 05 The Coupled Design Problem.md How each pillar constrains the others 6. Conclusion 06 Conclusion.md Why Vibe's architecture is defined by these constraints Core Thesis Leverage makes exploit resistance non negotiable. Low cap markets cannot depend on continuous synchronous matching. A solver or designated counterparty solves bootstrap, but introduces capital and yield requirements. Capital efficiency is therefore not a nice to have; it is what keeps the bootstrap layer alive. Vibe should be understood as a system designed around these three pillars rather than as a simple perp venue. Source Based primarily on 13 transcripts/13 transcript.md , with supporting context from the broader Vibe research set on order books, market bootstrap, listing economics, and LP structure. Version 1.0 — March 2026

chunks.title: Game Theory of Listings
chunks.text: Game Theory of Listings Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/00 abstract Source Draft Game Theory of Listings Abstract This paper argues that many of crypto's recurring structural failures originate in the game theory of listings . Any exchange, protocol, or listing committee that bears reputational cost, capital cost, or explicit gatekeeping power cannot list based on actual realized market demand. It can only list based on perceived interest : predicted user demand, expected volume, modeled safety, and social proof. That distinction is the root of a wide range of industry distortions. The central framework is simple. On one axis sits perceived interest . On the other sits actual interest . The latter can only be known after the market exists; before that point, it is fundamentally uncertain. Curated systems must therefore rely on proxies. They look for metrics, narratives, traction signals, deposits, and reputational assurances. Projects respond by optimizing for those signals. The result is a market that increasingly rewards listability rather than real evolutionary discovery. This logic helps explain phenomena such as low float high FDV launches, metric gaming, exchange listing deposits, meme coin migration, and the growing disconnect between token creation and serious market access. Zero cost

chunks.title: Game Theory of Listings
chunks.text: evolutionary discovery. This logic helps explain phenomena such as low float high FDV launches, metric gaming, exchange listing deposits, meme coin migration, and the growing disconnect between token creation and serious market access. Zero cost permissionless systems like early DEX environments preserve more noise and more failure, but they also preserve the possibility of discovering the assets that no central authority could have rationally selected in advance. The implication for Vibe is strategic. If the industry's missing layer is permissionless perpetual access, then perps must be brought to the zero perceived interest arena : the trenches where future unicorns begin. Vibe's role is not merely to list more assets. It is to restore evolutionary openness to derivative markets by making perpetual access possible before centralized or high cost systems are willing to validate a token. Version 1.0 — March 2026

chunks.title: Introduction
chunks.text: Introduction Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/01 introduction Source Draft Introduction Crypto likes to talk about market efficiency, decentralization, and permissionless innovation. But one of the industry's deepest bottlenecks is much simpler: who gets listed, where, and under what conditions . Listings are not passive infrastructure. They are selection mechanisms. They decide which assets gain visibility, which assets receive liquidity, which assets gain legitimacy, and which assets are left in the shadows. Because of that, listing systems shape the trajectory of the entire industry. The common mistake is to think of listings as a binary question of centralized versus decentralized. The more important distinction is structural: zero cost permissionless versus costly or curated . If a system imposes high stake requirements, reputational review, security deposits, or explicit committee judgment, then it is no longer allowing pure market evolution. It is introducing a filter. That filter may be understandable, rational, and even protective. But it still changes outcomes. This paper explores that logic through a simple claim: listing venues with meaningful cost or curation must optimize for perceived interest , not actual interest . Since actual interest can only be observed after the market has been allowed to exist, curated

chunks.title: Introduction
chunks.text: simple claim: listing venues with meaningful cost or curation must optimize for perceived interest , not actual interest . Since actual interest can only be observed after the market has been allowed to exist, curated systems are forced to rely on predictions and proxies. That creates powerful second order effects throughout crypto. The result is a landscape where: projects optimize for appearances and metrics venues avoid assets that might embarrass them retail discovery gets pushed into the least curated corners the future winners of the industry often begin where no serious venue is willing to look The rest of this paper develops that framework and shows why Vibe's long term role is to bring perpetuals to exactly that neglected edge. Back to Overview Next: Perceived vs Actual Interest →

chunks.title: Perceived vs Actual Interest
chunks.text: Perceived vs Actual Interest Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/02 perceived vs actual interest Source Draft Perceived vs Actual Interest The core distinction in the game theory of listings is between perceived interest and actual interest . Figure takeaway: the most important region is often left of the listing threshold. That is where actual future demand may be real, but the perceived signal is still too weak for curated venues to justify taking the risk. Actual interest means what the market really does once the asset is live: how much real buying power appears how much real selling pressure exists whether traders keep returning whether the asset becomes culturally or financially relevant The problem is that none of this can be known with certainty in advance. Before a token is listed, every exchange or listing committee is stuck with prediction. It can estimate, model, infer, and rank. But it cannot observe the thing itself because the market has not yet been allowed to form. Actual interest is only measurable after the information has been created by the market process. That means any authority deciding on a listing must use substitutes: community size social engagement deposits or fees volume expectations

chunks.title: Perceived vs Actual Interest
chunks.text: Actual interest is only measurable after the information has been created by the market process. That means any authority deciding on a listing must use substitutes: community size social engagement deposits or fees volume expectations narrative momentum institutional credibility These may be useful heuristics, but they are still heuristics. And once they become important, projects begin optimizing for them. The system gradually shifts from discovering genuine demand to rewarding signals that look like demand. This is the key epistemic limit at the center of the paper: actual interest is evolutionary and revealed over time; perceived interest is administrative and guessed ahead of time . Curated listing systems do not fail because their participants are malicious. They fail because they must act under uncertainty while bearing downside for being wrong. ← Introduction Next: The Cost of Curation →

chunks.title: The Cost of Curation
chunks.text: The Cost of Curation Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/03 the cost of curation Source Draft The Cost of Curation Once a venue bears meaningful downside from bad listings, its incentives change. Figure takeaway: permissionless venues inherit more noise, but curated venues inherit asymmetric blame. That asymmetry is what pushes them toward higher thresholds, more filtering, and more interference with open market evolution. A centralized exchange can lose reputation if a token performs disastrously. A high cost on chain listing venue can face backlash if staked governance approves obviously weak assets. A protocol that requires capital deposits or committee review creates a user expectation that someone, somewhere, is screening quality on their behalf. That expectation matters. The moment users believe there is curation, they also believe there is protection. And the moment a venue is expected to protect users, it has to behave conservatively. It cannot simply let everything evolve naturally. It must intervene. That produces a chain of effects: 1. Venues raise listing thresholds. 2. Projects try to look safer, larger, and more in demand than they really are. 3. Metrics become targets and are therefore gamed. 4. Listings shift toward assets with obvious short term perceived demand.

chunks.title: The Cost of Curation
chunks.text: thresholds. 2. Projects try to look safer, larger, and more in demand than they really are. 3. Metrics become targets and are therefore gamed. 4. Listings shift toward assets with obvious short term perceived demand. 5. The market loses exposure to the weird, early, uncertain assets that could have become the biggest winners. This is why cost matters so much. Even if a system is technically decentralized, a sufficiently high listing cost can recreate the behavior of a permissioned committee. Fewer actors may hold the gatekeeping power, but gatekeeping still exists. The issue is not simply centralization in the political sense. It is interference with evolutionary market discovery . That helps explain practices that otherwise seem arbitrary: massive security deposits, selective onboarding, reputational vetoes, and extremely conservative listing menus. These are not random decisions. They are the predictable result of a venue trying to defend itself against the downside of being wrong. ← Perceived vs Actual Interest Next: The Evolutionary Edge →

chunks.title: The Evolutionary Edge
chunks.text: The Evolutionary Edge Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/04 the evolutionary edge Source Draft The Evolutionary Edge Zero cost permissionless systems look chaotic from the outside, but that chaos has an important function: it preserves evolutionary openness. When anyone can create or list an asset with minimal friction, the market is flooded with noise. Most assets fail. Many should fail. Some are unserious, manipulative, or forgettable. But inside that noise sits something curated systems systematically struggle to capture: the future winner that looked unimportant before the market formed around it . This is the zone of zero perceived interest. That is where the unicorns tend to begin: before institutions care before large venues see enough signal before a token can justify expensive listing requirements before the crowd agrees it is important Curated systems mostly live downstream from that moment. They arrive after some combination of social proof, market cap, and trading history has already been established. They are good at scaling what looks validated. They are bad at discovering what cannot be validated ex ante. This is why the trenches remain so important in crypto culture. They are not just a meme. They are the last area where participants

chunks.title: The Evolutionary Edge
chunks.text: They are bad at discovering what cannot be validated ex ante. This is why the trenches remain so important in crypto culture. They are not just a meme. They are the last area where participants still feel they have access to raw evolutionary upside before selection committees and capital filters compress the opportunity set. The cost of this openness is obvious: more scams, more noise, more failure, more volatility. But the benefit is equally obvious: more genuine discovery. From a game theoretic perspective, zero cost permissionless listing systems do not solve the quality problem by predicting better. They refuse to solve it administratively at all. They push responsibility outward to users and let market selection do the work over time. ← The Cost of Curation Next: The Disconnect in Crypto →

chunks.title: The Disconnect in Crypto
chunks.text: The Disconnect in Crypto Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/05 the disconnect in crypto Source Draft The Disconnect in Crypto The modern crypto market contains a growing structural disconnect. On one side, token creation has become radically permissionless. Launchpads, bonding curve systems, and DEX environments can produce huge numbers of new assets with near zero friction. On the other side, serious market access remains scarce. Centralized exchanges list only a tiny number of assets. High cost venues list only what they expect to monetize safely. Derivative markets remain even more selective. This creates a two world system: millions of tokens can be born only a tiny minority can access deep liquidity, leverage, or institutional attention That gap is not just operational. It changes behavior across the entire industry. Projects begin designing themselves around what curated venues want to see. Users migrate toward memes and trenches because that is where unpriced upside still exists. Tokens jump from zero cost creation environments into increasingly filtered and expensive market layers. By the time a large venue is willing to list an asset, much of the open discovery phase is already over. This is one reason low float high FDV launches became so

chunks.title: The Disconnect in Crypto
chunks.text: expensive market layers. By the time a large venue is willing to list an asset, much of the open discovery phase is already over. This is one reason low float high FDV launches became so common. If serious listings are scarce, expensive, and highly selective, then teams are pushed to maximize perceived prestige and valuation before real market discovery has occurred. The listing system rewards the appearance of success in advance. The result is an industry where creation is abundant but validation is bottlenecked. And because perpetuals sit near the top of the market structure stack, the bottleneck becomes even more severe there. Spot access may be open. Serious shorting, hedging, and two sided price discovery are not. ← The Evolutionary Edge Next: Vibe and Perps for the Trenches →

chunks.title: Vibe and Perps for the Trenches
chunks.text: Vibe and Perps for the Trenches Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/06 vibe and perps for the trenches Source Draft Vibe and Perps for the Trenches This is where Vibe becomes strategically important. If the industry's next missing primitive is permissionless perpetual access, then derivatives cannot remain trapped inside the same listing logic that governs centralized exchanges and high cost curated venues. Perps must reach the part of the market where perceived interest is still near zero. That is what it means to bring perps to the trenches . Seen through the matrix above, the trenches are the far left side of the perceived interest axis: the area that curated venues structurally underserve because the reputational and economic case for listing is still too weak. The trenches are not just small cap speculation. They are the arena where: perceived interest is still minimal actual interest has not yet been revealed future unicorns are most likely to be invisible to listing committees no serious venue can justify early validation through traditional curation Vibe changes the equation by letting derivative access arrive earlier in the lifecycle. That does not mean every token becomes good. It means the market gains the ability

chunks.title: Vibe and Perps for the Trenches
chunks.text: can justify early validation through traditional curation Vibe changes the equation by letting derivative access arrive earlier in the lifecycle. That does not mean every token becomes good. It means the market gains the ability to test, hedge, short, and price assets before a centralized authority is comfortable endorsing them. This has several consequences: 1. Tokens no longer need to wait for elite approval before receiving two sided price discovery. 2. The long tail gains access to a stronger validation mechanism than spot trading alone. 3. Market evolution becomes less dependent on the predictions of centralized listing filters. 4. Crypto regains part of the open discovery process that made the industry exciting in the first place. In that sense, Vibe is not simply a listing venue. It is an anti bottleneck layer. It closes the gap between permissionless token creation and permissionless derivative discovery. If Binance and other large venues live far to the right on the perceived interest axis, then Vibe's job is to build infrastructure at the far left: the place where nobody is sure yet, where interest looks absent, and where the next major asset class or cultural phenomenon may still be forming. ← The Disconnect in Crypto Next: Conclusion →

chunks.title: Conclusion
chunks.text: Conclusion Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/07 conclusion Source Draft Conclusion The game theory of listings explains why so many crypto outcomes feel distorted without requiring bad actors to explain them. If a venue bears cost, reputation, or gatekeeping responsibility, it must list according to perceived interest rather than actual realized interest. That pushes the market toward prediction, curation, and interference. Projects learn to optimize for appearance. Venues learn to avoid embarrassment. The market becomes safer in one dimension, but less evolutionary in another. Zero cost permissionless systems make the opposite trade off. They tolerate more noise and more failure in exchange for preserving genuine discovery. That is why they remain the birthplace of so many unexpected winners. The strategic importance of Vibe follows from this directly. If crypto wants a genuinely permissionless derivative layer, it cannot wait until tokens are already obvious, validated, and institutionally legible. It must bring perpetuals into the zero perceived interest zone where future demand is still hidden. That is the real thesis of the paper: listing design is not just an exchange policy. It is a theory of market evolution. ← Vibe and Perps for the Trenches Back to Overview

chunks.title: Game Theory of Listings
chunks.text: Game Theory of Listings Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/11 gametheory of listings/11 docs/overview Source Draft Game Theory of Listings Paper Overview This paper argues that one of crypto's deepest structural problems comes from the way markets are listed. Any exchange, protocol, or committee that faces meaningful listing cost, reputational exposure, or stake based gatekeeping cannot optimize for actual interest . It can only optimize for perceived interest . That difference sounds subtle, but it explains a large share of industry behavior: why centralized venues over select for safe, obvious listings why projects game metrics to appear list worthy why low float high FDV launches emerged so easily why meme markets became the last venue for retail style discovery why there is a massive disconnect between token creation and perp access The paper frames listing design as a game theoretic problem. Curated systems try to protect users and preserve reputation, but in doing so they interfere with the natural evolutionary path of markets. Zero cost permissionless systems do the opposite: they allow more noise, but they also preserve the possibility of discovering assets whose future interest was impossible to predict in advance. Table of Contents Section File Description Abstract 00 Abstract.md Executive summary

chunks.title: Game Theory of Listings
chunks.text: the opposite: they allow more noise, but they also preserve the possibility of discovering assets whose future interest was impossible to predict in advance. The Evolutionary Edge 04 The Evolutionary Edge.md Why zero cost permissionless systems discover unicorns 5. The Disconnect in Crypto 05 The Disconnect in Crypto.md Millions of tokens created, very few get serious market access 6. Vibe and Perps for the Trenches 06 Vibe and Perps for the Trenches.md Bringing derivatives to zero perceived interest markets 7. Conclusion 07 Conclusion.md Why listing design determines industry evolution Core Thesis Listings are not neutral infrastructure. They are market shaping filters. Any listing system with cost or curation must act on predicted demand, not actual realized demand. That prediction pressure causes projects to optimize for signals, optics, and perceived safety. Zero cost permissionless systems preserve more evolutionary noise, but also more genuine discovery. Vibe matters because it extends market access into the part of crypto where

chunks.title: Game Theory of Listings
chunks.text: projects to optimize for signals, optics, and perceived safety. Zero cost permissionless systems preserve more evolutionary noise, but also more genuine discovery. Vibe matters because it extends market access into the part of crypto where perceived interest is lowest and future upside is hardest to predict. chunks.title: Abstract
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: Abstract Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/12 case study symm lp/12 docs/00 abstract Source Draft Abstract This case study analyzes a single liquidity provider (LP) deployment on Vibe for the SYMM market. The objective is to evaluate whether a project aligned token holder can use a Vibe vault deposit as a hedge and income strategy. The tracked test case consists of three SYMM deposits by LafaChief, totaling 2,271,131 tokens. At a deposit time price of $0.01280 per token, this corresponds to $29,070.4768 notional. The current token balance is 2,501,328.4 SYMM, a net increase of 230,197.4 tokens ( +10.14% ) versus tokens deposited. Using the canonical current SYMM price of $0.0074 , passive SYMM hold over the same window is 42.19% , while LP cash based performance is reported as $5,895.90 realized profit and $7,999.32 unrealized profit in USDC ( $13,895.22 combined, +47.80% on initial notional). The case suggests three core mechanisms: 1. perps market bootstrap collateral on Vibe can earn from fees, funding, and directional imbalance; 2. LP deposits can function as a partial hedge for project owners and whales already long token exposure; 3. once two sided trader flow deepens, LP collateral may be less operationally needed while fee/funding participation can remain

chunks.title: Abstract
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: deposits can function as a partial hedge for project owners and whales already long token exposure; 3. once two sided trader flow deepens, LP collateral may be less operationally needed while fee/funding participation can remain attractive. Key caveat: this is one case in one market regime and may not generalize. A complete evaluation requires audited trade level data, drawdown analysis, and out of sample periods with different market regimes.

An important signal in this case is that the outcomes were achieved in a low volume environment. The core driver was directional crowding (many users chose one side and were wrong), not high churn from frequent opening/closing of positions. In higher volume, higher fluctuation markets, fee revenue can add on top of funding and user PnL transfer dynamics. Why It Matters The strategic claim is that Vibe enables communities to bootstrap derivatives markets where: the team/whales/holders can contribute inventory as bootstrap LP capital, users can express directional views via perps, team/whales/holders can partially hedge downside while retaining most of their core token stack. Main Caveats this is a single case study; sample period is favorable for LP outcomes; unrealized PnL is path dependent and can reverse; results depend on position skew, leverage, and user trading behavior. chunks.title: Performance and Unit Economics
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: Forward implication: in markets with higher fluctuation and higher open/close activity, fee revenue can stack on top of the same funding and user PnL channels. 3.5 Metric Sign Convention (Critical) Per your protocol accounting definitions: Current Debt is user debt to LP that is already realized. Current UPnL is users' current unrealized PnL. If either field is negative , that means the LP is in profit on that component. Operationally: LP realized component = Current Debt LP unrealized component = Current UPnL Example from the provided screenshot: Current Debt = 5,895.90 LP has about $5,895.90 realized user debt in its favor. Current UPnL = 7,999.32 LP has about $7,999.32 unrealized edge at that mark. Current Debt is realized and withdrawable for the LP, while Current UPnL remains mark to market and is not guaranteed as immediately withdrawable cash. 3.6 Revenue Attribution (Qualitative) The narrative states that the dominant share of LP revenue came from trader side losses in a long skew/down price regime, with additional contribution from funding, fees, and liquidations. Interpretation: this indicates a strong directional imbalance regime; strategy sensitivity to crowding direction is high; persistence of this split is uncertain across regimes. 3.7 Measurement Gaps to Close A production grade case study should add: vault level time series of NAV and PnL attribution, realized vs unrealized split by

chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study

chunks.title: Risk Analysis and Edge Cases
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: Risk Analysis and Edge Cases Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/12 case study symm lp/12 docs/04 risk analysis and edge cases Source Draft Risk Analysis and Edge Cases 4.1 Core Risk Statement The source speaker explicitly notes that outcomes could have differed if traders had won instead of lost. This is the central risk: LP returns are not one way; they are regime dependent. At the provided dashboard mark, both Current Debt and Current UPnL are negative, which is LP favorable under your sign convention. This strengthens the case outcome but does not remove forward regime risk. 4.2 Principal Risk Vectors 1. Directional reversal risk If trader crowding and price direction align against LP positioning, LP profitability can compress or turn negative. 2. Unrealized PnL volatility risk Marked gains can change quickly; unrealized profits ( Current UPnL ) are not secured until they move into realized debt/cash outcomes. 3. Leverage amplification risk High user leverage can increase both fee/funding opportunities and tail loss velocity. 4. Liquidity stress risk In thin markets, liquidation and slippage dynamics can deteriorate quickly in fast moves. 5. Concentration risk Heavy dependence on one asset/community behavior can create fragile outcomes. 4.3 Hedge Logic and Residual Exposure The case argues

chunks.title: Risk Analysis and Edge Cases
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: In thin markets, liquidation and slippage dynamics can deteriorate quickly in fast moves. 5. Concentration risk Heavy dependence on one asset/community behavior can create fragile outcomes. 4.3 Hedge Logic and Residual Exposure The case argues that insiders can reduce downside concern by depositing only a small share of total token holdings (for example 5 20%) and keeping the majority uncommitted. This creates a split exposure: retained token stack preserves upside participation, LP allocation monetizes trading activity and directional imbalances. Residual risk remains if market conditions change sharply or if user profitability rises persistently. 4.4 Edge Cases to Test Before Scaling prolonged uptrend with profitable long traders; chop regime with low directional edge but persistent fees; sudden gap move with thin order book; community activity decay reducing open interest and turnover. 4.5 Governance and Transparency Requirements To present this as institutional grade evidence, publish: methodology for PnL attribution, explicit sign convention dictionary for all dashboard fields, vault accounting policy for realized/unrealized treatment, scenario based stress test assumptions, clear disclosures on survivorship and selection bias.

Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/12 case study symm lp/12 docs/05 scaling and replication framework Source Draft Scaling and Replication Framework 5.1 Scaling Hypothesis The transcript suggests potential scalability from tens of thousands to higher notional LP sizes, conditional on trader appetite, leverage usage, and sustained market activity. This is plausible, but should be tested using phased scaling rather than one step capital expansion. 5.2 Replication Conditions for Other Communities For another token community to replicate this model, the following conditions are likely required: active trader base willing to express views via perps, credible market narrative and recurring participation, sufficient liquidity/risk controls for liquidation integrity, transparent LP reporting that builds depositor trust. 5.3 Practical Deployment Playbook 1. Pilot phase start with small LP allocation from treasury/insiders; monitor directional skew, funding transfer, and turnover quality. 2. Validation phase produce weekly PnL attribution and drawdown reports; verify that returns are not solely one off directional luck. 3. Controlled scale up increase capital in predefined tranches; use stop conditions tied to drawdown, skew instability, and liquidity quality. 4. Steady state operation maintain target utilization bounds; keep treasury exposure limits and emergency controls documented. 5.4 KPI Stack for a Beta Report net ROI

chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: stop conditions tied to drawdown, skew instability, and liquidity quality. 4. Steady state operation maintain target utilization bounds; keep treasury exposure limits and emergency controls documented. 5.4 KPI Stack for a Beta Report net ROI vs token hold benchmark realized ROI vs total marked ROI max drawdown and recovery time fee/funding/liquidation revenue split open interest concentration and leverage distribution capacity stress metrics at higher TVL tiers 5.5 Replication Risk This specific sample appears to benefit from a strong long skew/down price period. Communities should assume weaker returns in neutral or adverse regimes and test whether economics remain attractive after conservative stress adjustments.

chunks.title: Conclusion
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: Conclusion Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/12 case study symm lp/12 docs/06 conclusion Source Draft Conclusion The SYMM LP case provides a credible example of Vibe's bootstrap LP model working in a real market context. The tracked test consists of three deposits by LafaChief totaling 2,271,131 SYMM. Using an average deposit price of $0.01280 , this corresponds to $29,070.4768 initial notional. At the canonical current SYMM price of $0.0074 , passive SYMM hold over the same window is 42.19% ( $12,264.1074 ), while reported LP outcomes are $5,895.90 realized profit and $7,999.32 unrealized profit ( $13,895.22 combined, +47.80% on initial notional). These results are explained by trader crowding, adverse price movement for long biased users, funding transfers, and fee/liquidation flows. A key finding is that these outcomes were achieved in a low volume environment ( $100,000 total, $1,000 average daily volume). That suggests meaningful LP return potential can exist even without high trading turnover; in higher volume markets, fee driven upside can potentially layer on top. The most important strategic insight is not the headline APR figure. It is the mechanism: a project aligned participant can allocate a limited share of token inventory as bootstrap LP capital, keep most of the stack for

chunks.title: Conclusion
chunks.track: 12 - First Vibe Perp Vault Case study
chunks.text: important strategic insight is not the headline APR figure. It is the mechanism: a project aligned participant can allocate a limited share of token inventory as bootstrap LP capital, keep most of the stack for directional upside, and potentially earn USDC denominated yield from trader activity and imbalance. However, this remains one favorable period case. To establish durability, the next version should include audited on chain attribution, regime diverse backtesting, and explicit drawdown/risk benchmarks. In short: this single test is a strong proof of mechanism and a promising go to market template for token communities, but still needs full cycle evidence before being treated as a generalized performance standard.

chunks.title: Vibe Trading: Proof of Value
chunks.text: Vibe Trading: Proof of Value Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/13 framework value permissionless perps/13 docs/00 abstract Source Draft Vibe Trading: Proof of Value A Framework for Understanding Economic Value Creation in Permissionless Perpetual Markets Abstract Permissionless perpetual protocols face a fundamental question: do they create sustainable economic value for all participants—liquidity providers, traders, and projects—or do they rely on transient incentives that collapse under stress? This paper presents a rigorous proof of value for Vibe Trading's hybrid architecture, demonstrating how it creates measurable, durable value while avoiding the structural failures of alternative designs. We make three primary contributions: First , we introduce a value dimension framework covering four constituencies: LPs (token depositors), traders, projects, and the ecosystem. We show how Vibe's design aligns incentives across these groups rather than pitting them against each other. Second , we document the LP value proposition in detail: 70% revenue share to token depositors, approximately 100x capital efficiency versus USDC vault based protocols, and risk alignment that allows token holders to earn yield on assets they already hold. Critically, Vibe does not require external USDC LPs—the solver self funds stablecoin operations, eliminating the 50–80% APR demand that makes USDC vault protocols economically unsustainable for low cap

chunks.title: Vibe Trading: Proof of Value
chunks.text: earn yield on assets they already hold. Critically, Vibe does not require external USDC LPs—the solver self funds stablecoin operations, eliminating the 50–80% APR demand that makes USDC vault protocols economically unsustainable for low cap perpetuals. Third , we establish comparative advantage against two alternative paradigms: (i) USDC vault protocols, which face unsustainably high LP yield demands and capital inefficiency; and (ii) token margined (inverse) protocols, which suffer from reflexivity, death spirals, and LP lose lose equilibria. Vibe's USDC margined hybrid with token inventory vaults and active solver risk management represents the structurally superior path. Early validation supports the thesis: 160+ launch partners have committed millions in token value prior to full product launch, based on the design and value proposition alone. This proof of value provides a foundation for evaluating Vibe's economic sustainability and long term viability. Keywords Proof of Value, Liquidity Provision, Capital Efficiency, Token Vaults, USDC Margined Perpetuals, Risk Alignment, Value Creation, LP Economics, Permissionless Derivatives Paper Structure Section Title Description 1 Introduction What proof of value means and why it matters 2 Framework Value dimensions: LP, Trader, Project, Ecosystem 3 LP Value Proposition 70% share, capital efficiency, risk alignment 4 Trader and Project Value Perp access, hedging, project utility 5 Economic Clarity Risk waterfall, who bears what 6 Comparative Advantage vs USDC vault, vs token margined

chunks.title: Section 1: Introduction
chunks.text: Section 1: Introduction Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/13 framework value permissionless perps/13 docs/01 introduction Source Draft Section 1: Introduction 1.1 The Proof of Value Question Every protocol claims to create value. The critical question is: is the value real, measurable, and sustainable? For permissionless perpetual markets—especially for long tail, low cap assets—this question is acute. Multiple design paradigms exist: USDC vault protocols, token margined (inverse) protocols, and hybrid architectures. Each makes economic claims. Many fail under stress. Liquidity providers demand unsustainable yields, traders face illiquid markets, projects cannot afford listing costs, and protocols collapse or pivot. Proof of value means demonstrating that: 1. Value is created for each participant class (LPs, traders, projects) 2. Value is structurally aligned—participants benefit from, not in spite of, each other 3. The design is economically sustainable under realistic conditions 4. Early evidence supports the claims This paper applies this framework to Vibe Trading. 1.2 Why Proof of Value Matters For LPs Liquidity providers must assess whether depositing capital yields adequate risk adjusted returns. In perpetual protocols, LPs often bear counterparty risk, directional exposure, or both. If the economics are misaligned, LPs exit—and the protocol fails. For Traders Traders need execution quality, liquidity, and certainty that profits

chunks.title: Section 1: Introduction
chunks.text: adjusted returns. In perpetual protocols, LPs often bear counterparty risk, directional exposure, or both. If the economics are misaligned, LPs exit—and the protocol fails. For Traders Traders need execution quality, liquidity, and certainty that profits will be paid. If the protocol cannot sustainably pay winners (due to LP exit, bad debt, or structural failure), traders avoid it. For Projects Projects and token holders evaluate whether providing liquidity or supporting a perp market generates positive value. Exchange listings require stablecoin deposits; market makers require commitments. If the cost exceeds the benefit, projects abstain. For the Ecosystem The broader DeFi ecosystem benefits from protocols that create net positive value—capital efficiency, price discovery, hedging—rather than extracting value through unsustainable incentives or passing hidden risks to participants. 1.3 The Challenge of Low Cap Perpetuals Low cap token perpetuals present unique challenges: Capital efficiency : Full collateralization of potential payouts is prohibitively expensive. A $1M open interest position with 10x leverage implies $10M+ in potential payout. Who backs it? LP risk perception : External LPs (especially USDC depositors) perceive low cap perp risk as extreme. Observed yield demands: 50–80% APR . At that level, protocols cannot sustain operations—fees and spreads would need to exceed what traders will pay. Token margined pitfalls : Using the traded asset as collateral creates reflexivity: collateral and exposure move together.

chunks.title: Section 1: Introduction
chunks.text: . At that level, protocols cannot sustain operations—fees and spreads would need to exceed what traders will pay. Token margined pitfalls : Using the traded asset as collateral creates reflexivity: collateral and exposure move together. Price drops trigger margin collapse, liquidations cascade, and death spirals emerge. Historical precedent: Futureswap, Drift v1, Synthetix inverse synths. Oracle and manipulation risk : Low cap tokens have thin spot liquidity. Oracle manipulation and spot perp arbitrage become profitable. Protocols must design for attack economics, not just "normal" conditions. 1.4 Vibe's Approach: A Preview Vibe addresses these challenges through a hybrid architecture: 1. Token based vaults for inventory : Projects and token holders deposit the asset they already hold. Incremental risk is low; they earn 70% of market revenue. 2. Solver funded USDC : No external USDC LPs. The solver and protocol self fund stablecoin operations. Capital recycles rapidly; risk is tightly bounded through pre hedging. 3. Active risk management : Dynamic spreads, funding, and borrow rates; defense hierarchy (pricing → local insurance → global insurance → ADL); bell curve flattening across markets. 4. Lifecycle progression : Bootstrap (solver as counterparty) → Mature (trader to trader netting) → Graduate (order book). LP capital ignites markets; over time, reliance on LP capital decreases. This paper demonstrates that this approach creates measurable value and is structurally superior

chunks.title: Section 1: Introduction
chunks.text: counterparty) → Mature (trader to trader netting) → Graduate (order book). LP capital ignites markets; over time, reliance on LP capital decreases. This paper demonstrates that this approach creates measurable value and is structurally superior to alternatives. 1.5 Thesis Statement Vibe's hybrid architecture—token vault inventory plus solver funded USDC, with active risk management and lifecycle progression—creates sustainable economic value for LPs, traders, and projects. The design achieves approximately 100x capital efficiency versus USDC vault protocols, aligns risk with parties best positioned to bear it, and has attracted 160+ launch partners with millions in committed token value prior to full product launch. This constitutes a rigorous proof of value. 1.6 Paper Contributions Theoretical : Value dimension framework for perp protocol evaluation Risk alignment principles for LP design Comparative analysis of USDC vault vs. token vault vs. token margined Empirical : LP yield demand analysis (50–80% for USDC vault low cap perps) Capital efficiency comparison ( 100x advantage) Early validation metrics (160+ partners, millions deposited) Practical : Economic clarity: who bears risk, when, and how Sustainability criteria for protocol design DDQ aligned risk statement 1.7 Scope This paper focuses on economic value creation and structural sustainability . It does not cover: Smart contract security (separate audit domain) Regulatory compliance (jurisdiction dependent) Detailed mathematical derivations (see vibe full derivation) Next Section: A Framework

chunks.title: Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions
chunks.text: Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/13 framework value permissionless perps/13 docs/02 framework Source Draft Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions 2.1 The Four Constituencies Proof of value requires demonstrating that each participant class benefits. We identify four constituencies: Constituency Role Value Sought LPs (Token Depositories) Provide token inventory for perp markets Yield on held assets, ecosystem support Traders Open/close perp positions Execution, leverage, hedging Projects Token issuers, treasury managers Utility for token, market visibility Ecosystem Broader DeFi/crypto Capital efficiency, price discovery A protocol that creates value for some constituencies at the expense of others is unstable. Value must be aligned —each party benefits from the participation of others. 2.2 Value Alignment 2.2.1 Positive Sum Design In a positive sum design: More traders → more fees → more LP revenue → more LPs → deeper markets → better execution → more traders More projects → more markets → more diversity → more traders → more fees → more projects benefit LP deposits → markets can bootstrap → traders get access → projects get perp listings → ecosystem gains Vibe's alignment : Token depositors earn 70% of revenue; traders get perp access; projects get utility without stablecoin

chunks.title: Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions
chunks.text: deposits → markets can bootstrap → traders get access → projects get perp listings → ecosystem gains Vibe's alignment : Token depositors earn 70% of revenue; traders get perp access; projects get utility without stablecoin commitment; ecosystem gets permissionless perps for long tail assets. 2.2.2 Risk Alignment Value alignment requires risk alignment : risk should flow to parties best positioned to bear it. Risk Type Who Bears It (Vibe) Why Aligned Directional (token price) Token depositors Already hold token; incremental exposure minimal Counterparty (trader wins) Solver + LP vault capacity Solver hedges; vault backs residual Execution (hedge slippage) Solver Operational expertise Liquidation shortfall Insurance → ADL Escalation path defined USDC deployment Solver Self funded; short cycle recycling Misalignment example : USDC vault protocols ask stablecoin holders to bear directional risk on low cap tokens. Stablecoin holders are structurally averse to this risk—hence 50–80% yield demands. Vibe avoids this by using token holders as inventory providers. 2.3 Value Metrics 2.3.1 LP Value Metrics Revenue share : 70% to token depositors Capital efficiency : 100x vs. USDC vault (estimated) Yield expectation : Modest (comparable to staking); no 50%+ demand Risk adjusted return : Incremental risk on existing exposure 2.3.2 Trader Value Metrics Market access : Perps for tokens that otherwise have none Execution quality : Solver provides liquidity; spreads reflect risk Payout

chunks.title: Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions
chunks.text: 50%+ demand Risk adjusted return : Incremental risk on existing exposure 2.3.2 Trader Value Metrics Market access : Perps for tokens that otherwise have none Execution quality : Solver provides liquidity; spreads reflect risk Payout certainty : Defined counterparty; risk waterfall documented Lifecycle : Bootstrap (wide spreads) → Mature (tighter) → Graduate (order book) 2.3.3 Project Value Metrics No stablecoin commitment : Deposit tokens, not USDC No market maker hire : Solver handles execution Revenue share : 70% of market fees to depositors Ecosystem support : Deepen markets, add utility, signal commitment Alternative cost : Compare to CEX listing fees, MM arrangements, Uniswap single sided (effective sell pressure) 2.3.4 Ecosystem Value Metrics Capital efficiency : Token inventory vs. full USDC collateralization Permissionless listing : Any token can have perp market Price discovery : Two sided markets (long + short) Lifecycle completion : Connects fair launch to institutional (see Listing Monopoly paper) 2.4 Sustainability Criteria For value to be durable, the protocol must satisfy: 1. LP sustainability : LPs earn sufficient risk adjusted yield without protocol subsidy 2. Trader sustainability : Execution quality and payout certainty maintained 3. Solver sustainability : Hedge economics allow profitable operation 4. No Ponzi dynamics : Revenue from real trading, not new LP deposits 5. Stress resilience : Defense hierarchy handles volatility; ADL as last resort

chunks.title: Section 2: A Framework for Value Dimensions
chunks.text: maintained 3. Solver sustainability : Hedge economics allow profitable operation 4. No Ponzi dynamics : Revenue from real trading, not new LP deposits 5. Stress resilience : Defense hierarchy handles volatility; ADL as last resort 2.5 Summary The value framework has four dimensions (LP, Trader, Project, Ecosystem), requires alignment across them, and demands risk allocation to appropriate parties. Vibe's design scores well on each dimension. The following sections provide detail on LP value (Section 3), Trader and Project value (Section 4), economic clarity (Section 5), and comparative advantage (Section 6). Next Section: LP Value Proposition →

chunks.title: Section 3: LP Value Proposition
chunks.text: Section 3: LP Value Proposition Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/13 framework value permissionless perps/13 docs/03 lp value proposition Source Draft Section 3: LP Value Proposition 3.1 The Core Design Insight Vibe separates two distinct liquidity roles: 1. USDC liquidity : For settlement, hedging, cross chain operations 2. Token liquidity : For perp market inventory (covering long/short exposure) These roles have fundamentally different risk profiles. Vibe assigns each to the party best positioned to bear it. 3.2 The USDC Side: Solver Funded, Not LP Funded 3.2.1 No External USDC LPs Vibe does not ask external LPs to deposit USDC. The stablecoin liquidity required for settlement and hedging is self funded by the protocol and the solver . 3.2.2 How It Works The solver's USDC usage functions as short term deployment : capital deployed on one chain is typically recovered on another within a short cycle. Because the solver can pre hedge before accepting exposure, risk on these flows is tightly bounded. Implications : Extremely high capital efficiency on the USDC side (rapid recycling, not locking) No need for large external stablecoin pools Internal yield on solver deployed USDC believed to exceed alternative deployments System self sustaining without external USDC LPs 3.2.3 Why Closed

chunks.title: Section 3: LP Value Proposition
chunks.text: the USDC side (rapid recycling, not locking) No need for large external stablecoin pools Internal yield on solver deployed USDC believed to exceed alternative deployments System self sustaining without external USDC LPs 3.2.3 Why Closed USDC Pool? An external USDC LP would need to trust the operator entirely and would demand yield commensurate with perceived risk—lending stablecoins to a leveraged low cap perp protocol is high risk. Keeping the pool protocol operated avoids this mismatch. 3.3 Why USDC Vault Protocols Face 100x Capital Efficiency Disadvantage 3.3.1 The Problem Protocols relying on external USDC vault deposits to back leveraged perpetuals on low cap tokens face a structural issue: perceived risk . 3.3.2 Observed LP Yield Demands When presented with depositing USDC to provide leverage for low cap perps, institutional and sophisticated capital providers assess risk as extremely high . Protocols have observed LP yield demands in the range of 50–80% annualized —economically unsustainable to serve. 3.3.3 The Feedback Loop Higher offered leverage → deeper USDC backing required Deeper backing → higher yield demand Higher yield demand → higher fees/spreads required Higher fees → traders avoid the market Thin volume → protocol fails 3.3.4 The Capital Efficiency Estimate Vibe's modeling and game theoretical analysis estimates that token based vaults achieve approximately 100x greater capital efficiency compared to USDC vault based low cap

chunks.title: Section 3: LP Value Proposition
chunks.text: market Thin volume → protocol fails 3.3.4 The Capital Efficiency Estimate Vibe's modeling and game theoretical analysis estimates that token based vaults achieve approximately 100x greater capital efficiency compared to USDC vault based low cap perpetual protocols. The core reason: Vibe eliminates the need to compensate external LPs for bearing risk they are structurally poorly suited to assess or manage. 3.4 The Token Side: Aligning Risk with Holders Who Already Bear It 3.4.1 The Observation Token holders are already directionally exposed. A holder of Token X bears the full price risk of that asset. The incremental risk of depositing tokens into a Vibe vault—where tokens back a perp market on that same asset—is materially lower than the risk they already accepted by holding. 3.4.2 Natural Alignment Factor Implication Token holders want utility Staking, single sided LP offer limited yield; Vibe provides additional yield on held assets Token holders support ecosystem Founders, early investors, whales want to add utility and deepen markets Cost basis often low Many depositors acquired at fraction of current value; committing a small % for yield is favorable Yield expectations modest Unlike USDC depositors demanding 50%+ APR, token holders satisfied with incremental yield (staking level) Key principle : Vibe allocates risk to parties already carrying that risk and for whom incremental exposure is smallest. 3.5 Revenue Sharing:

chunks.title: Section 3: LP Value Proposition
chunks.text: USDC depositors demanding 50%+ APR, token holders satisfied with incremental yield (staking level) Key principle : Vibe allocates risk to parties already carrying that risk and for whom incremental exposure is smallest. 3.5 Revenue Sharing: 70% to Token Depositors/Projects To incentivize token side LP participation, Vibe distributes 70% of market revenue to projects and token depositors. This is designed to establish market dominance in permissionless perp listings by making economics unambiguously attractive. Observation : The team's conviction—supported by behavior—is that most projects would provide liquidity even without the profit share . Motivations (ecosystem support, token utility, community signaling, low incremental risk) are sufficient. The revenue share sweetens an already aligned deal. 3.6 LP Value Summary Table Dimension USDC Vault Protocols Vibe (Token Vaults + Solver Funded USDC) Who provides stablecoins External LPs Solver / protocol (self funded) Who provides token inventory N/A or protocol funded Token holders / projects USDC LP yield demand 50–80%+ APR (unsustainable) Not applicable Token LP yield expectation N/A Modest (staking comparable) Capital efficiency (est.) Baseline 100x improvement LP risk alignment Stablecoin holders bear directional risk they don't want Token holders bear incremental risk on exposure they already hold Revenue share to LPs/projects Varies 70% of market revenue 3.7 LP Profit Decomposition (Technical) From the vibe full derivation specification, LP profit per market: Where: Rev m

chunks.title: Section 3: LP Value Proposition
chunks.text: bear incremental risk on exposure they already hold Revenue share to LPs/projects Varies 70% of market revenue 3.7 LP Profit Decomposition (Technical) From the vibe full derivation specification, LP profit per market: Where: Rev m = trading fees, spread, funding, liquidation fees, borrow revenue Cost m = hedge cost, external borrow, operations α = counterparty share (Phase 1: 1, Phase 2: 0 as netting increases) Π m traders = aggregate trader PnL Π m hedge = solver hedge PnL L m shortfall = bad debt / liquidation shortfall As markets mature, netting increases, α decreases, and LP risk exposure declines while revenue persists. chunks.title: Section 4: Trader and Project Value
chunks.text: Section 4: Trader and Project Value Draft status: imported from the primary markdown source. Needs editorial restructuring, cross links, and source note polish before final publication. Sources https://0xneelo.github.io/vibe docs/docs/13 framework value permissionless perps/13 docs/04 trader and project value Source Draft Section 4: Trader and Project Value 4.1 Trader Value 4.1.1 Market Access The primary value for traders: perpetual markets exist where none did before. Tokens in the $70K–$100M market cap range typically have no perp access. Vibe creates these markets permissionlessly. Use cases : Speculation : Leveraged exposure to trending tokens Hedging : Token holders protect against downside Arbitrage : Spot perp, cross venue Yield : Funding rate capture (long/short based on skew) 4.1.2 Execution Quality Solver provided liquidity : No need to wait for counterparty; async execution Dynamic pricing : Spreads and funding reflect risk; tighter in calm, wider in stress Lifecycle improvement : Bootstrap (wider spreads) → Mature (tighter) → Graduate (order book) 4.1.3 Payout Certainty Traders need assurance that profits will be paid. Vibe provides: Defined counterparty : Solver (and LP vault capacity) backs positions Documented risk waterfall : Pricing → local insurance → global insurance → ADL No ADL by default : Solver hedges; ADL only when defenses exhausted 4.1.4 "Vote No" Capability Uniswap and PumpFun enable "voting yes" (buying). Derivatives enable "voting no" (shorting). Two

chunks.title: Section 4: Trader and Project Value
chunks.text: insurance → global insurance → ADL No ADL by default : Solver hedges; ADL only when defenses exhausted 4.1.4 "Vote No" Capability Uniswap and PumpFun enable "voting yes" (buying). Derivatives enable "voting no" (shorting). Two sided conviction improves price discovery. Traders gain the ability to express bearish views on any token with a Vibe market. 4.2 Project Value 4.2.1 The Project Problem Projects and token treasuries face limited productive uses for tokens: Exchange listings : Require stablecoin deposits Market makers : Require stablecoin commitments Uniswap single sided LP : Functions as effective sell pressure Passive holding : Zero yield Vibe inverts this: projects deposit the asset they already hold and want to support , earn revenue, and create a leveraged trading market. 4.2.2 What Projects Get Benefit Description Perp market Token gains derivative exposure; visibility and utility 70% revenue share Fees flow to depositors No stablecoin commitment Use tokens, not scarce USDC No MM hire Solver handles execution Ecosystem support Deepen markets, signal commitment to community Low incremental risk Deposit small % of holdings; already directionally exposed 4.2.3 Alternative Cost Comparison Alternative Cost / Friction CEX listing Application, fees, compliance, stablecoin deposit Market maker Retainer, spread agreement, stablecoin commitment Uniswap single sided Effective sell pressure; impermanent loss risk Do nothing No perp, no yield on treasury Vibe offers: deposit tokens,

chunks.text: CEX listing Application, fees, compliance, stablecoin deposit Market maker Retainer, spread agreement, stablecoin commitment Uniswap single sided Effective sell pressure; impermanent loss risk Do nothing No perp, no yield on treasury Vibe offers: deposit tokens, earn 70% of fees, get perp market. No stablecoins, no MM, no CEX dependency. 4.2.4 Project Motivations (Beyond Revenue) Even without profit share, projects have incentives to participate: Utility : Perp market adds use case for token Community : Holders want to trade/speculate; project enables it Signaling : "We have a perp" = legitimacy Treasury deployment : Idle tokens earn yield The 70% share makes the economics unambiguous; the underlying alignment makes participation rational without it. 4.3 Ecosystem Value 4.3.1 Permissionless Perps Any token can have a perp market. This fills the lifecycle gap (see Listing Monopoly paper) and connects fair launch ecosystems with institutional infrastructure. 4.3.2 Capital Efficiency Token inventory + solver funded USDC achieves 100x capital efficiency vs. USDC vault. Capital that would otherwise sit idle or demand 50%+ yield is deployed productively. 4.3.3 Price Discovery Two sided markets (long + short) improve price discovery versus spot only. Hedging availability reduces volatility and manipulation risk for the broader ecosystem. 4.3.4 Lifecycle Continuity Vibe bridges the gap between DEX graduation and CEX/order book perps. Tokens flow: creation → DEX → Vibe perps → order
